title: Ear fullness
population: Adults aged over 18 presenting with hearing difficulties or hearing loss.
Children aged 6 months to 12 years with tympanostomy tubes or being considered for tubes for otitis media.
Children and young people with acute otitis media.
People aged 2 years or older with diffuse acute otitis externa.
settings: Any care setting involved in paediatric otitis-media assessment or tympanostomy-tube follow-up.
Health services caring for children and young people with acute otitis media.
Primary, community, audiology, and secondary care.
Primary, emergency, paediatric, internal-medicine, and otolaryngology settings.

heading_path: AAO-HNSF Updated Clinical Practice Guideline: Acute Otitis Externa – Press Release & Fact Sheet
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: What is Acute Otitis Externa? Acute Otitis Externa (AOE), commonly known as “swimmer’s ear,” is one of the most common infections encountered by clinicians, affecting 2.4 million people (1 in 123) annually in the United States, with regional variations based on age and geography. AOE is an infection of the outer ear, usually caused by bacteria that multiply when water becomes trapped in the ear ca

Clinical Practice Guideline: Tympanostomy Tubes in Children (Update)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: The purpose of this clinical practice guideline is to reassess and update recommendations in the prior guideline from 2013 and to provide clinicians with trustworthy, evidence-based recommendations on patient selection and surgical indications for managing tympanostomy tubes in children. This guideline is intended for any clinician involved in managing children, aged 6 months to 12 years, with tym

NICE guideline Reference number: NG91 Published: 28 March 2018 Last updated: 11 March 2022 Download guidance (PDF)
1.1 Managing acute otitis media 1.2 Choice of treatment

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that: acute otitis media is a self-limiting infection that mainly affects children acute otitis media can be caused by viruses and bacteria, and it is difficult to distinguish between these (both are often present at the same time) symptoms last for about 3 days, but can last for up to 1 week most children and young people get better within 3 days without antibiotics complications such as

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Assess and manage children under 5 who present with fever as outlined in the NICE guideline on fever in under 5s . [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give advice about the usual course of acute otitis media (about 3 days, can be up to 1 week). [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer regular doses of paracetamol or ibuprofen for pain. Use the right dose for the age or weight of the child at the right time, and use maximum doses for severe pain. [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider eardrops containing an anaesthetic and an analgesic for pain (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ) if: an immediate oral antibiotic prescription is not given (see recommendations 1.1.8 to 1.1.14 ), and there is no eardrum perforation or otorrhoea. Review treatment if symptoms do not improve within 7 days or worsen at any time. [2022]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Explain that evidence suggests decongestants and antihistamines do not help symptoms. [2018]

heading_path: All children and young people with acute otitis media
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Reassess at any time if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, taking account of: alternative diagnoses, such as otitis media with effusion (glue ear) any symptoms or signs suggesting a more serious illness or condition previous antibiotic use, which may lead to resistant organisms. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider no antibiotic prescription or a back-up antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ), taking account of: evidence that antibiotics make little difference to symptoms (no improvement in pain at 24 hours, and after that the number of children improving is similar to the number with adverse effects) evidence that antibiotics make little difference to the develo

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When no antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about: an antibiotic not being needed seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, do not start to improve after 3 days, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018]

heading_path: Children and young people who may be less likely to benefit from antibiotics (those not covered by recommendations 1.1.11 to 1.1.14)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When a back-up antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about: an antibiotic not being needed immediately using the back-up prescription if symptoms do not start to improve within 3 days or if they worsen rapidly or significantly at any time seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018] For a short expla

heading_path: Children and young people who may be more likely to benefit from antibiotics (those of any age with otorrhoea or those under 2 years with infection in both ears)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider no antibiotic prescription with advice (see recommendation 1.1.9), a back-up antibiotic prescription with advice (see recommendation 1.1.10) or an immediate antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ), taking account of: evidence that acute complications such as mastoiditis are rare with or without antibiotics possible adverse effects of antibiotics, partic

heading_path: Children and young people who may be more likely to benefit from antibiotics (those of any age with otorrhoea or those under 2 years with infection in both ears)
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When an immediate antibiotic prescription is given, give advice about seeking medical help if symptoms worsen rapidly or significantly, or the child or young person becomes systemically very unwell. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committee made these recommendations and how they might affect practice, see the evidence and committee discussion on no antibiotic , back-up antibiotics and c

heading_path: Children and young people who are systemically very unwell, have symptoms and signs of a more serious illness or condition, or are at high risk of complications
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer an immediate antibiotic prescription (see recommendation 1.2.1 for choice of treatment ) with advice (see recommendation 1.1.12). [2018]

heading_path: Children and young people who are systemically very unwell, have symptoms and signs of a more serious illness or condition, or are at high risk of complications
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer children and young people to hospital if they have acute otitis media associated with: a severe systemic infection (see the NICE guidelines on suspected sepsis in under 16s and suspected sepsis in people aged 16 or over ) acute complications, including mastoiditis, meningitis, intracranial abscess, sinus thrombosis or facial nerve paralysis. [2018] For a short explanation of why the committe

Follow table 1 when prescribing treatment for children and young people with acute otitis media. [2018, amended 2022] Table 1 Treatment for children and young people under 18 years Treatment Choice, dosage and course length Eardrops containing an anaesthetic and an analgesic Phenazone 40 mg/g with lidocaine 10 mg/g : Apply 4 drops two or three times a day for up to 7 days Use only if an immediate

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: NICE guideline Reference number: NG98 Published: 21 June 2018 Last updated: 02 October 2023 Download guidance (PDF)

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: 1.1 Assessment and referral 1.2 Removing earwax 1.3 Investigation using MRI 1.4 Treating idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss 1.5 Assessment and management in audiology services 1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices 1.7 Follow-up in audiology services 1.8 Information and support Terms used in this guideline

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off‑label use), professional guidelines, stan

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing difficulties or suspected hearing difficulties
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For adults who present for the first time with hearing difficulties, or in whom you suspect hearing difficulties: exclude impacted wax and acute infections such as otitis externa, then arrange an audiological assessment (for more information, see recommendation 1.5.1 ) and refer for additional diagnostic assessment if needed (see the recommendations on sudden or rapid onset of hearing loss and hea

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Sudden or rapid onset of hearing loss
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer adults with sudden onset or rapid worsening of hearing loss in one or both ears, which is not explained by external or middle ear causes, as follows. If the hearing loss developed suddenly (over a period of 3 days or less) within the past 30 days, refer immediately (to be seen within 24 hours) to an ear, nose and throat service or an emergency department. If the hearing loss developed sudden

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing loss with specific additional symptoms or signs
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer immediately (to be seen within 24 hours) adults with acquired unilateral hearing loss and altered sensation or facial droop on the same side to an ear, nose and throat service or, if stroke is suspected, follow a local stroke referral pathway. For information about diagnosis and initial management of stroke, see the NICE guideline on stroke and transient ischaemic attack in over 16s .

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing loss with specific additional symptoms or signs
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Refer immediately (to be seen within 24 hours) adults with hearing loss who are immunocompromised and have otalgia (ear ache) with otorrhoea (discharge from the ear) that has not responded to treatment within 72 hours to an ear, nose and throat service.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing loss with specific additional symptoms or signs
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider making a referral to an ear, nose and throat service using a suspected cancer pathway referral for adults of Chinese or south-east Asian family origin who have hearing loss and a middle ear effusion not associated with an upper respiratory tract infection.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing loss with specific additional symptoms or signs
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring adults with hearing loss that is not explained by acute external or middle ear causes to an ear, nose and throat, audiovestibular medicine or specialist audiology service for diagnostic investigation, using a local pathway, if they present with any of the following: unilateral or asymmetric hearing loss as a primary concern hearing loss that fluctuates and is not associated with

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Hearing loss with specific additional symptoms or signs
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring adults with hearing loss to an ear, nose and throat service if, after initial treatment of any earwax (see the recommendations on removing earwax ) or acute infection, they have any of: partial or complete obstruction of the external auditory canal that prevents full examination of the eardrum or taking an aural impression pain affecting either ear (including in and around the e

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Adults with suspected or diagnosed dementia, mild cognitive impairment or a learning disability
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring adults with diagnosed or suspected dementia or mild cognitive impairment to an audiology service for a hearing assessment because hearing loss may be a comorbid condition.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Adults with suspected or diagnosed dementia, mild cognitive impairment or a learning disability
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring adults with diagnosed dementia or mild cognitive impairment to an audiology service for a hearing assessment every 2 years if they have not previously been diagnosed with hearing loss.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
Adults with suspected or diagnosed dementia, mild cognitive impairment or a learning disability
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referring people with a diagnosed learning disability to an audiology service for a hearing assessment when they transfer from child to adult services, and then every 2 years.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer to remove earwax for adults in primary care or community ear care services if the earwax is contributing to hearing loss or other symptoms, or needs to be removed in order to examine the ear or take an impression of the ear canal.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Do not offer adults manual syringing to remove earwax.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider ear irrigation using an electronic irrigator, microsuction or another method of earwax removal (such as manual removal using a probe) for adults in primary or community ear care services if: the practitioner (such as a community nurse or audiologist): has training and expertise in using the method to remove earwax is aware of any contraindications to the method the correct equipment is av

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When carrying out ear irrigation in adults: use pre-treatment wax softeners, either immediately before ear irrigation or for up to 5 days beforehand if irrigation is unsuccessful: repeat use of wax softeners or instil water into the ear canal 15 minutes before repeating ear irrigation if irrigation is unsuccessful after the second attempt, refer the person to a specialist ear care service or an ea

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise adults not to remove earwax or clean their ears by inserting small objects, such as cotton buds, into the ear canal. Explain that this could damage the ear canal and eardrum, and push the wax further down into the ear.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer MRI of the internal auditory meati to adults with hearing loss and localising symptoms or signs (such as facial nerve weakness) that might indicate a vestibular schwannoma or CPA (cerebellopontine angle) lesion, irrespective of pure tone thresholds.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider MRI of the internal auditory meati for adults with sensorineural hearing loss and no localising signs if there is an asymmetry on pure tone audiometry of 15 dB or more at any 2 adjacent test frequencies, using test frequencies of 0.5, 1, 2, 4 and 8 kHz.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.4 Treating idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider a steroid to treat idiopathic sudden sensorineural hearing loss in adults.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.5 Assessment and management in audiology services
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Include and record the following as part of the audiological assessment for adults: a full history including relevant symptoms, comorbidities, cognitive ability, physical mobility and dexterity the person's hearing and communication needs at home, at work or in education, and in social situations any psychosocial difficulties related to hearing the person's expectations and motivations with respec

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.5 Assessment and management in audiology services
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: After the audiological assessment: discuss with the person: the pure tone audiogram and the impact their hearing loss might have on communication hearing deficits (such as listening in noisy environments) that are not obvious from the audiogram options for managing their hearing needs, such as acoustic or bone conduction hearing aids, assistive listening devices and communication strategies, and t

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.5 Assessment and management in audiology services
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give the person and, if they wish, their family or carers, information about: the causes of hearing loss, how hearing loss affects the ability to communicate and hear, and how it can be managed organisations and support groups for people with hearing loss.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer hearing aids to adults whose hearing loss affects their ability to communicate and hear, including awareness of warning sounds and the environment, and appreciation of music.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer 2 hearing aids to adults with aidable hearing loss in both ears. Explain that wearing 2 hearing aids can help to make speech easier to understand when there is background noise, make it easier to tell where sounds are coming from, and improve sound quality.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider using motivational interviewing or engagement strategies and goal setting when discussing hearing aids with adults for the first time, to encourage acceptance and use of hearing aids.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Show the hearing aids when they are first offered and discuss their suitability with the person.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
Prescribing and fitting hearing aids for adults
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: When prescribing and fitting hearing aids, explain the features on the hearing aid that can help the person to hear in background noise, such as directional microphone and noise reduction settings.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
Prescribing and fitting hearing aids for adults
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Advise adults with hearing aids about choosing microphone and noise reduction settings that will meet their needs in different environments, and ensure that they know how to use them.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
Prescribing and fitting hearing aids for adults
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give adults with hearing aids information about getting used to hearing aids, cleaning and caring for their hearing aids, and troubleshooting.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Give adults with hearing loss information about assistive listening devices such as personal loops, personal communicators, TV amplifiers, telephone devices, smoke alarms, doorbell sensors, and technologies such as streamers and apps.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Tell adults with hearing loss about organisations that can demonstrate and provide advice on how to obtain assistive listening devices, such as social services, the fire service, or the government through programmes such as Access to Work or Disabled Student Allowance.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
1.6 Hearing aids, assistive listening devices and implantable devices
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Cochlear implants are recommended as an option in NICE technology appraisal guidance for treating severe to profound deafness in some people who do not receive adequate benefit from acoustic hearing aids. For full details, see the guidance on cochlear implants (TA566, 2019) . See NICE's interventional procedures guidance on auditory brain stem implants for deafness caused by damage to the vestibul

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Offer adults with hearing aids a face-to-face follow‑up audiology appointment 6 to 12 weeks after the hearing aids are fitted, with the option to attend this appointment by telephone or electronic communication if the person prefers.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: At the follow‑up audiology appointment for adults with hearing aids: ask the person if they have any concerns or questions address any difficulties with inserting, removing or maintaining their hearing aids provide information on communication, social care or rehabilitation support services if needed tell the person how to contact audiology services in the future for aftercare, including repairs a

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For adults with hearing loss in both ears who chose a single hearing aid, consider a second hearing aid at the follow‑up appointment.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: For adults with hearing loss who have chosen a management strategy other than hearing aids, such as assistive listening devices or communication strategies, offer a follow‑up appointment when the effectiveness of the device or strategy can be evaluated.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Tell adults with hearing loss who have chosen not to have a hearing aid or other device how to contact audiology services in the future.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Consider having a system in place for recalling people with hearing devices for regular reassessment of their hearing needs and devices.

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Follow the principles on tailoring healthcare services for each person and enabling people to actively participate in their care in the NICE guideline on patient experience in adult NHS services by, for example: taking into account the person's ability to access services and their personal preferences when offering appointments taking measures, such as reducing background noise, to ensure that the

heading_path: Hearing loss in adults: assessment and management
locator.paragraph_or_text_span: Person to receive a diagnosis or ruling out of cancer within 28 days of being referred urgently by their GP for suspected cancer. For further details, see NHS England's webpage on faster diagnosis of cancer .
